claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with the basics:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children - Alex who is 16, and Blake who is 5. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have two children - Alex (16) and Blake (5). Let me ask you some questions to assess eligibility for each of them.

Let's start with **Alex, who is 16**:

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs, or similar courses)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is in sixth form doing A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you! Alex being in sixth form doing A-levels means they're in approved education.

Now let me ask about your living situation:

**Do both Alex and Blake live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, in Manchester. Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't live with me - Alex lives with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Blake (5) lives with you in Manchester, but Alex (16) lives with their other parent.

**For Alex specifically - do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep (such as paying for food, clothes, pocket money, or other expenses)? And if so, are you contributing at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be?**

(Child Benefit is currently £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child and £16.95 per week for additional children)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep. I help out with clothes, school supplies, and give money for their expenses. I contribute about £50 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Contributing £50 a week is above the Child Benefit amount, which is good to know.

Now, a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Has anyone else (such as Alex's other parent) already claimed Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex's other parent hasn't claimed Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now let me ask about Blake:

**Since Blake (5) lives with you, are you responsible for looking after Blake on a day-to-day basis?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake does live with me, but their other parent is also very involved. Actually, their other parent already claims Child Benefit for Blake and lives with Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Just to make sure I understand correctly:

**You mentioned Blake lives with you, but you're also saying Blake's other parent lives with Blake and claims Child Benefit for them. Does Blake actually live with their other parent, or is there a shared living arrangement? I want to make sure I have the correct living situation for Blake.**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Sorry, let me clarify - Blake actually lives with their other parent, not with me. Their other parent has Blake living with them and claims the Child Benefit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. So to confirm:

- **Alex (16)**: Lives with their other parent, you contribute £50/week towards their upkeep, and no one has claimed Child Benefit for Alex
- **Blake (5)**: Lives with their other parent, who already claims Child Benefit for Blake

Just one final question before I can give you my assessment:

**Are you and the other parent (or parents if different people) married or in a civil partnership and living together?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, we're not together. The children's other parent and I are separated, and we live in different households.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on the information you've provided, here's my determination:

### **Alex (16 years old): ✅ ELIGIBLE**
You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Alex is 16 and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form), which qualifies them for Child Benefit until they turn 20 or leave education
- Although Alex doesn't live with you, you're contributing £50 per week towards their upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold (£25.60/week)
- No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Under the rules, you can qualify for Child Benefit when you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after the child

### **Blake (5 years old): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**
You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake
- Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child
- Two people cannot receive Child Benefit for the same child

**Next steps:** If you wish to claim Child Benefit for Alex, you should make a claim. You may also want to be aware of the High Income Child Benefit tax charge if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000.

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and in approved education (doing A-levels at sixth form), which means they qualify as a child for Child Benefit purposes (children under 20 in approved education are covered). Although Alex does not live with you, you are contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount (£25.60/week for an eldest/only child). Under the rules, you can still qualify for Child Benefit if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after the child. Additionally, no one else has claimed Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 5 years old and lives with their other parent, who is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Under the rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child. Since Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake. Two people cannot get Child Benefit for the same child. Therefore, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children in separate households. For Alex (16), the claimant is eligible because Alex is in approved education, the claimant contributes sufficient financial support (£50/week, exceeding the Child Benefit threshold), and no one else claims for Alex. For Blake (5), the claimant is not eligible because Blake's other parent already claims Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim per child.

Although Alex does not live with you, you are contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount (£25.60/week for an eldest/only child). Under the rules, you can still qualify for Child Benefit if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after the child. Additionally, no one else has claimed Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 5 years old and lives with their other parent, who is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Under the rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child. Since Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake. Two people cannot get Child Benefit for the same child. Therefore, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children in separate households. For Alex (16), the claimant is eligible because Alex is in approved education, the claimant contributes sufficient financial support (£50/week, exceeding the Child Benefit threshold), and no one else claims for Alex. For Blake (5), the claimant is not eligible because Blake's other parent already claims Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim per child.
